a.body: That backlog sounds expensive in team time. I work with RelayDesk. It puts customer messages into one shared queue so teams can review the workflow before deciding on a pilot: https://example.com/relaydesk/pilot
a.rationale: Acknowledges the stated burden before offering a relevant next step.

b.body: One option is to consolidate the incoming messages before changing staffing. I work with RelayDesk, which organizes customer messages in one shared queue. The workflow is here: https://example.com/relaydesk/pilot
b.rationale: Leads with the workflow change and keeps the product claim within the profile.

a.body: Three inboxes make ownership hard to see. I work with RelayDesk. It gives the team one shared queue, and you can review the workflow here: https://example.com/relaydesk/pilot
a.rationale: Reflects the operating problem without promising a result.

b.body: A shared queue may be the simplest first test. I work with RelayDesk, which organizes customer messages in one place. Here is the pilot workflow: https://example.com/relaydesk/pilot
b.rationale: Starts with the requested utility and uses an approved product claim.

a.body: For five people, I would start by checking how ownership and handoffs work before comparing feature lists. I work with RelayDesk. You can review its shared-queue workflow here: https://example.com/relaydesk/pilot
a.rationale: Answers the evaluation need before introducing the product.

b.body: RelayDesk is built around one shared customer-message queue. I work with the team, so treat that as disclosed context. The pilot workflow is here if it fits your five-person setup: https://example.com/relaydesk/pilot
b.rationale: Provides a direct fit statement while making affiliation explicit.
